description: Bearer-prefix dispatch contract per ADR 0016. v0.3 broadens what an `Authorization: Bearer` header may carry — session tokens (v0.1), share tokens (v0.2 / ADR 0012), and personal access tokens (v0.3). Adopters that compose multiple bearer types in one middleware MUST classify each candidate by its prefix and dispatch to the matching verifier. The `auth.kind` audit discriminator returns one of {`pat`, `share`, `session`}; sessions are the default for any bearer that does NOT carry a registered Flametrench prefix (session tokens have no spec-pinned prefix — they are opaque strings, often random or JWT-shaped). Bearer types do NOT overlap: a `pat_…` string MUST NOT be retried against the session resolver if PAT verification fails, and vice versa. The classification is what every conforming SDK MUST produce identically — interop depends on byte-equivalent prefix dispatch.

tests.description: A `pat_<32hex>_<…>` bearer routes to the PAT verifier.
A `shr_<…>` bearer routes to the share verifier (ADR 0012).

Session tokens have no spec-pinned prefix — adopters typically use opaque random strings. Any bearer that does not match a registered prefix (`pat_`, `shr_`) routes to the session resolver. The classifier MUST default to `session`; the session resolver itself rejects invalid sessions with InvalidTokenError.
A JWT-shaped bearer (three base64url-encoded segments, dot-separated) routes to `session`. The PAT classifier MUST NOT consume tokens just because they happen to start with letters before a dot.

Critical no-cross-routing invariant: a malformed `pat_…` string (here, missing the secret segment) MUST classify as `pat` and let the PAT verifier reject it. The classifier MUST NOT fall back to the session resolver — that would mask the real error and silently mis-categorize the audit `auth.kind`.
Same invariant for share tokens — a `shr_…` string with broken structure classifies as `share` and lets the share verifier reject it. Cross-routing is forbidden.
